data.title: 3 Australian Growth Stocks With High Insider Ownership Worth A Closer Look
data.description:
With inflation concerns, higher bond yields, and energy driven price pressures in focus, many investors are looking for stocks where management and analysts are aligned on growth potential. Our Fast Growing Stocks With High Insider Ownership screener highlights companies where insiders hold substantial stakes and outlooks are described as optimistic, which can signal confidence in the business plan and future prospects. In this article, you will see 3 stocks from this screener that stand out on growth and insider alignment, giving you a focused starting list for further research in a market that is still shaped by rates, inflation, and geopolitics.

Advertisement

Predictive Discovery (ASX:PDI)

Overview: Predictive Discovery is an Australian based gold company focused on exploring, developing and operating gold assets in West Africa, centred on its large-scale Bankan Gold project in north east Guinea. Through its operating mines and development projects, Predictive Discovery aims to turn a sizeable resource base into long term gold production.

Market Cap: A$3.31b

Predictive Discovery provides exposure to a West African gold story where analysts see long term revenue and earnings potential, but the company is still loss making and carrying clear funding and execution risks. The merger with Robex, ramp up at Kiniero and progress at Bankan together point to a broader production base and free cash flow that is already being directed into future growth. Recent production updates highlight high throughput and solid recoveries. At the same time, the short cash runway, reliance on higher risk borrowing, past shareholder dilution and recent insider selling all raise questions about how the next phase will be financed. Understanding how these pieces fit together is important before forming a clear view on Predictive Discovery.

Predictive Discovery’s push to convert a large West African resource into long term production could be more finely balanced than it appears, and the 2 key rewards and 4 important warning signs (2 are major!) might highlight a twist in that story investors are missing.

Telix Pharmaceuticals (ASX:TLX)

Overview: Telix Pharmaceuticals develops and sells radiopharmaceuticals that help doctors image and treat cancers, pairing diagnostic scans with targeted radiation therapies for prostate, kidney, brain and other solid tumors across major markets including the United States, Europe and Asia.

Operations: Telix generates most of its roughly US$804.3m in revenue from Precision Medicine imaging products (about US$621.9m) and Manufacturing Solutions (about US$245.1m), with smaller contributions from Therapeutics and sales heavily concentrated in the United States at about US$784.9m.

Market Cap: A$5.05b

Telix Pharmaceuticals sits at the intersection of cancer imaging and therapy, with Illuccix and Gozellix already generating substantial revenue while late stage programs like TLX591 and TLX250 move through Phase 3 trials in prostate and kidney cancer. Recent updates on ProstACT Global and LUTEON, plus a new Melbourne manufacturing facility and the Regeneron partnership, point to a company investing heavily to control its supply chain and expand its product reach. At the same time, Telix remains loss making, carries higher risk funding, faces pricing pressure in PSMA imaging and depends on successful trial outcomes and regulatory decisions. For investors, the mix of strong commercial traction and high clinical, regulatory and funding risk makes the full Telix story worth a closer look.

Telix Pharmaceuticals is already generating substantial revenue from cancer imaging, but the real question is how the next wave of therapies could reshape the story. Get the full context with the analysis report for Telix Pharmaceuticals

GemLife Communities Group (ASX:GLF)

Overview: GemLife Communities Group develops, builds, owns and operates resort style land lease communities for over 50s across Australia, combining new home sales with long term community management, maintenance and lifestyle facilities for residents.

Operations: GemLife Communities Group generates most of its A$281.7m in revenue from Development at A$259.8m, with A$21.9m coming from Community Operations, all within Australia.

Market Cap: A$1.67b

GemLife Communities Group sits at the crossroads of Australia’s growing downsizer trend and a long pipeline of over 50s communities. Its vertically integrated model helps it manage build costs and keep margins in focus while developing and operating 33 communities. Recurring site rental fees that track CPI or a fixed 3.5% floor can provide a more predictable income base on top of development earnings. However, the reliance on higher risk borrowing, weaker cash flow coverage and gearing that could rise to 25% to 35% leave it exposed if credit tightens or rates stay elevated. Combined with a relatively new board and recent one off losses, GemLife presents a mix of growth potential and execution risk that may warrant closer consideration when assessing its role in a portfolio focused on fast growth and insider alignment.

data.title: CME Group (CME) Stock Faces Dividend Coverage Questions Despite 62.7% Net Margin Reinforcing Bull Case
data.description:
CME Group (CME) has just posted its Q2 2026 numbers with revenue of US$1,706.2 million and basic EPS of US$2.89, set against a trailing twelve month backdrop of US$6.76 billion in revenue and EPS of US$11.77. The company has seen quarterly revenue move between US$1,534.4 million and US$1,876.2 million over the last six reported periods, while basic EPS has ranged from US$2.49 to US$3.25, giving investors a clear read on earnings power heading into this latest update. With trailing net margins at 62.7%, this quarter focuses on how CME Group is converting its revenue base into profit and what that implies for the durability of those margins.

See our full analysis for CME Group.

With the headline figures on the table, the next step is to compare these results with the widely followed narratives around CME Group's growth, profitability and income profile to assess which views are supported and which are challenged by the latest numbers.

See what the community is saying about CME Group

Advertisement

Margins stay high at 62.7%

  • Over the last 12 months, CME Group converted US$6.76 billion of revenue into US$4.24 billion of net income, giving it a net margin of 62.7% compared with 57.9% a year earlier.
  • Consensus narrative highlights CME Group’s strong demand for risk management and growing international and retail activity, and the current 62.7% net margin gives numbers behind that story, even as:
    • Five year earnings have grown 12% per year with the latest year at 13.8%, which lines up with the idea of a business that has been handling higher volumes efficiently.
    • At the same time, analysts expect margins to ease to 60.9% over three years, so the current margin level sits above what the consensus narrative is building into its longer term model.

High margins are a big part of why many investors watch CME Group closely, since they show how much of every US$1 of revenue is keeping for shareholders.

Earnings growth vs slower forecasts

  • On a trailing basis, earnings of US$4.24 billion and EPS of US$11.77 reflect 13.8% earnings growth over the past year and about 12% per year over five years, while forward estimates point to around 5.4% annual earnings growth and 5.5% revenue growth.
  • Analysts' consensus view sees strong historical growth giving CME Group a solid base, yet the forecast step down to mid single digit growth creates a contrast that matters for expectations:
    • Supporters of the bullish narrative point to record contract activity, new products and global expansion as drivers that helped deliver the multi year 12% earnings growth rate.
    • Those same forecasts now building in roughly 5.4% annual earnings growth and EPS of US$13.42 by 2029 imply the market is already tempering that bullish pace in its models.

For you as an investor, the key question is whether CME Group’s past double digit earnings growth or the mid single digit forecasts feel more representative of what you expect from the business over time.

Bulls argue that CME Group’s mix of high margins and global volume growth could support a stronger case than these mid single digit forecasts suggest, especially if key contracts stay active and new products keep gaining traction. 🐂 CME Group Bull Case

P/E discount and dividend coverage watch

  • CME Group trades on a trailing P/E of 21.3x compared with 39x for the US Capital Markets industry and 26.2x for peers, while offering a 4.55% dividend yield that is not well covered by free cash flow on a trailing 12 month view.
  • Critics in a more bearish narrative focus on the mix of modest forecast growth and cash flow demands, and the current numbers give some backing to those concerns:
    • The trailing P/E below both industry and peer averages lines up with the idea that the market is not paying a premium multiple even after several years of earnings growth.
    • The 4.55% yield, combined with weaker free cash flow coverage, means income focused investors may want to look closely at how much headroom exists if growth stays around the forecast 5.4% and 5.5% ranges.

If you are weighing that trade off between valuation, growth and income, it helps to compare CME Group’s 21.3x P/E and 4.55% yield with what you can find in other capital markets stocks and in wider income oriented opportunities. 🐻 CME Group Bear Case

data.title: Maase (MAAS) Expands Its AI Ties, Is The Premium Valuation Already Too Rich?
data.description:
Maase (NasdaqGM:MAAS) is back in focus after its subsidiary Huazhi Future agreed with Zhongchuang Liankong to build a customized enterprise AI platform powered by the Lingyan Miaoyu large language model.

See our latest analysis for Maase.

These AI product launches and enterprise partnerships have arrived alongside sharp share price swings, with Maase’s stock falling 17.46% on a 1 day share price return but still delivering an 81.12% 90 day share price return and 183.26% year to date share price return. This suggests that momentum has cooled recently after a strong run.

If Maase’s AI push has caught your attention, it could be a good moment to see what else is emerging in the sector and look through 31 AI small caps

After Maase’s sharp pullback but strong year to date gain, the next step is weighing its US$8.8b market value and recent losses against what the AI story might justify, or considering whether the market’s caution is more reasonable.

Advertisement

Preferred Price-to-Book of 14.8x: Is It Justified for Maase?

Across traditional valuation checks, Maase currently screens as expensive, with a Price-to-Book (P/B) ratio of 14.8x versus 1.7x for the broader US Insurance sector and 4.4x for closer peers.

The P/B ratio compares the stock price to the accounting value of net assets on the balance sheet. A higher multiple often implies that investors are paying up for future growth, strong profitability, or valuable intangible assets. In Maase’s case, the company reports revenue of just $6.621 and a loss of $21.722, so the market is assigning a high valuation multiple to a business that is still small in revenue terms and currently unprofitable.

Compared with both its industry and peer group, Maase’s 14.8x P/B stands out as significantly higher than the US Insurance average of 1.7x and the 4.4x peer average. This indicates investors are willing to pay a much richer price for each dollar of book value than is typical in this space. With no fair ratio estimate or discounted cash flow figure available, there is limited model based context for where that multiple could settle if sentiment or expectations change, so the premium sits squarely in focus for anyone assessing the stock.

Result: Price-to-Book of 14.8x (OVERVALUED).

data.title: AAR (AIR) Stock Margin Jump Reinforces Bullish Earnings Narratives After FY 2026 Results
data.description:
AAR (AIR) has just posted FY 2026 results capped by fourth quarter revenue of US$928 million and basic EPS of US$1.29, against a backdrop of very large year over year reported earnings growth and a net profit margin that now sits at 5.7%. Over the past year the company has seen trailing twelve month revenue reach US$3.3 billion and EPS climb to US$4.90. Reported earnings growth of 1,401.6% reflects a sharp shift from the prior year’s base. For investors, this combination of higher earnings and a stronger margin profile sets the stage for a results season where profitability quality and sustainability are front of mind.

See our full analysis for AAR.

With the latest numbers on the table, the next step is to see how this earnings profile lines up with the prevailing narratives around AAR’s growth, risks, and long term margin story.

TTM earnings surge to US$187.7 million

  • On a trailing basis, AAR generated US$187.7 million of net income and US$4.90 in EPS on US$3.3b of revenue, compared with US$12.5 million of net income and US$0.35 EPS on US$2.8b of revenue a year earlier.
  • Consensus narrative highlights expansion of MRO capacity and parts distribution as key earnings drivers, and the numbers line up with that view:
    • Revenue over the last year is reported at US$3.3b, and the business has added new MRO capacity in Oklahoma City and Miami that is already fully sold out, which fits with the idea of stronger maintenance demand feeding into higher earnings.
    • Trailing revenue growth of 6.7% per year and the move in net margin to 5.7% from 0.4% are consistent with the consensus view that operational efficiencies and higher quality contracts are supporting a more profitable earnings mix.

Valuation sits between P/E and DCF signals

    • The lower P/E compared with industry and peers lines up with the view that MRO expansion, digital platforms such as Trax and government contracts can support earnings without paying the same multiple as some peers.
    • At the same time, the share price being above the DCF fair value of US$104.54 means anyone leaning on cash flow estimates needs to be comfortable that margin improvements and future revenue growth justify paying more than that model output.
For investors who want to see how other market participants connect these valuation signals to the broader story around AAR, it is worth checking what the community is debating right now: 📊 Read the what the Community is saying about AAR..

    • The warning that operating cash flow does not adequately cover debt means the company’s ability to fund MRO expansion, digital investments like Trax and acquisitions from internal cash generation alone is limited, which aligns with worries about financial flexibility.
    • With revenue reported at US$3.3b and earnings growth far above the 5.7% 5 year earnings CAGR, skeptics may question whether this level of profitability and spending can be maintained if commercial aviation cycles turn or OEM competition in aftermarket services intensifies.

data.title: RTX (NYSE:RTX) Lands $1.8 Billion Navy SPY 6 Radar Extension
data.description:
• RTX secured a $1.8b U.S. Navy contract extension for SPY-6 radar production and sustainment.
  • The agreement supports continued manufacturing, upgrades, and lifecycle support for the SPY-6 family of radars.
  • RTX plans to modernize facilities and aims to double SPY-6 output by 2028.

RTX (NYSE: RTX), trading at $194.88, continues to attract attention as a large defense contractor with long-running ties to U.S. military programs. The stock is up 26.5% over the past year and 149.5% over five years, reflecting ongoing investor interest in its defense and aerospace exposure. This new SPY-6 award adds to an already sizable backlog of government-related work.

For investors tracking RTX, the extended SPY-6 program represents a multi-year stream of radar-related revenue that sits alongside other defense contracts. The company’s planned facility upgrades and production ramp could affect future capital spending and margins, which are key factors to monitor as this contract progresses.

The SPY 6 extension ties directly into RTX’s core thesis as a long term defense electronics supplier. For you as an investor, this contract is less about a one off boost and more about reinforcing RTX’s role in radar and sensor systems that sit at the heart of U.S. Navy surface fleets. The agreement extends production and sustainment work on a radar family that is already fielded, which can add to contract visibility alongside RTX’s broader backlog. Planned facility upgrades and a larger engineering workforce point to higher near term capital and operating expenditure, so it will be important to see how efficiently RTX converts that investment into margins over the life of the program. Compared with peers such as Lockheed Martin, Northrop Grumman and General Dynamics, this kind of radar contract helps RTX stay relevant in high priority naval defense areas where performance, reliability and through life support often matter as much as initial unit pricing.

The company has seen revenue move from ¥32,431 million and EPS of ¥44.36 in Q1 2026 to ¥36,698 million and ¥53.01 respectively in Q1 2027. 56.2% net margin keeps OBICLtd’s profits thick

  • On a trailing basis, OBICLtd converted ¥139,476 million in revenue into ¥78,376 million of net income, which works out to a 56.2% net profit margin compared with 53.9% a year earlier.
  • Supporters note that these rich margins sit alongside earnings momentum, with profits up 16.4% over the past year and annualized earnings growth of 13.5% over five years, while investors still have to weigh that strength against comments that the dividend track record is unstable and therefore not a straightforward income story.
    • Supporters often point to this combination of a 56.2% margin and 16.4% profit growth as evidence of efficient operations that can absorb cost pressure while still lifting earnings.
    • On the other hand, the reference to an unstable dividend history contrasts with the high margin profile and reminds income focused investors that strong profits do not automatically translate into predictable cash returns.

Trailing EPS of ¥180.09 underpins the OBICLtd story

  • Over the last twelve months, OBICLtd generated trailing basic EPS of ¥180.09, supported by ¥78,376 million of net income on ¥139,476 million of revenue.
  • Supporters highlight this earnings base alongside the recent Q1 2027 EPS of ¥53.01, arguing that the business appears to have a steady earnings profile, and that view aligns with the data showing 16.4% profit growth over the past year and 13.5% per year over five years, while also sitting alongside forecasts that point to more moderate earnings growth of about 7.6% a year from here.
    • The shift from historical 13.5% annual earnings growth to forecast growth around 7.6% indicates that expectations are already a bit more conservative, which can appeal to investors who prefer results to exceed rather than chase aggressive projections.
    • At the same time, the trailing EPS of ¥180.09 and Q1 2027 EPS of ¥53.01 provide a current earnings base that any future growth rate will compound from, which is why some investors pay close attention to how stable that level looks over multiple quarters.

Curious how other investors are piecing together these profit trends with valuation and risk for OBICLtd, and what stories they think the numbers are telling, check out the Curious how numbers become stories that shape markets? Explore Community Narratives.

P/E of 22.9x with a ¥5,126.15 target in sight

  • At a share price of ¥4,162, OBICLtd trades on a P/E of 22.9x compared with 15.6x for the Japan IT industry and 56.9x for its peer group, while analysts’ average price target of ¥5,126.15 and a DCF fair value of ¥4,173.74 both sit above or very close to the current price.
  • Critics who take a more bearish stance often point to the premium to the Japan IT industry P/E and the comment that valuation is higher than the domestic industry average. That concern has to be set against the fact that the current price of ¥4,162 is only about 0.3% below analyst derived fair value and sits slightly below the DCF fair value of ¥4,173.74, which indicates a modest premium to the industry but not a stretched level relative to analyst and cash flow based benchmarks.
    • The roughly 23.2% upside implied by the ¥5,126.15 analyst target contrasts with the more modest 0.3% gap to analyst fair value, so readers need to treat targets and fair value estimates as different reference points rather than a single number.

The company has seen revenue move from US$3.1 billion in Q1 2025 to US$4.3 billion in Q2 2026, while quarterly EPS has ranged from US$1.14 to US$2.01 across that period, setting up a story of strong trailing profit metrics and firm margins that investors will weigh against more modest growth expectations.

Margins stay elevated at 35.9%

  • Over the last 12 months, Southern Copper converted US$15.8b of revenue into US$5.7b of net income, which works out to a 35.9% net profit margin compared with 30.4% a year earlier.
  • Bulls point to this high margin as support for their view that Southern Copper can keep generating strong profits, and the latest numbers give them some backing:
    • Trailing twelve month basic EPS sits at US$6.76 versus a five year compound earnings growth rate of 10.7% per year, while earnings over the last year are described as 56.9% higher than the five year average.
    • At the same time, the bullish narrative expects margins to rise further over time, so the current 35.9% margin is a key reference point investors can use when judging whether those expectations look reasonable.

data.description:
With inflation concerns, higher global yields and geopolitical tensions all pulling markets in different directions, many investors are looking for leaders who are directly invested in the outcome. Future (TSE:4722)

Overview: Future Corporation is a Tokyo based IT services group that helps companies design, build and run their technology systems, while also providing business innovation support across areas such as digital marketing, IT education and e commerce.

Operations: Future generates the bulk of its revenue from IT Consulting & Services at ¥68,522 million, with Business Innovation contributing ¥8,395 million and smaller amounts from other activities, almost all within Japan.

Market Cap: ¥195.6b

Future stands out in this founder led group because its core IT consulting business is paired with measurable fundamentals, including a net profit margin of around 15.7% and a dividend yield of 2.2%. Revenue and earnings forecasts that outpace the wider Japanese market, together with a P/E below peers and an estimated fair value that is moderately higher than the current price, may appeal to investors who focus on both growth and valuation. At the same time, the company relies entirely on external borrowing for funding, which raises questions about resilience if conditions tighten. The experienced board and management team help balance that risk, but investors need to look closer to judge how comfortable they are with this trade off.

Future’s mix of higher revenue and earnings forecasts, a P/E below peers, and an estimated fair value above the current price hints at a story the market has not fully priced in yet. The analyst forecasts for Future could reveal how that upside sits against its reliance on external borrowing and what that might mean next.

Rorze (TSE:6323)

Overview: Rorze is a Fukuyama based specialist in automation systems for semiconductor and flat panel display production, supplying robots, wafer handling equipment and control devices, alongside life science automation tools like cell incubators and handling systems to customers around the world.

Market Cap: ¥779.7b

Rorze attracts attention in the founder led universe because it sits at the heart of chip and display production. Earnings are forecast to grow around 21% a year and revenue is expected to rise faster than the wider Japanese market. Profitability is already solid, with a net profit margin of 16.5% and return on equity forecast to move from 14% toward the low 20% range. However, the stock trades on a relatively rich P/E and above some estimates of fair value. Add in a large recent one off loss, a funding structure that leans entirely on external borrowing and a volatile share price, and you have a business with powerful growth expectations but real questions about how that growth is being priced in.

Rorze’s accelerating role in chip and display automation is being weighed against rich expectations and that recent one off hit, so the 2 key rewards and 2 important warning signs (1 is major!) could show whether the growth story is quietly masking something investors are missing.

Sansan (TSE:4443)

Overview: Sansan is a Tokyo based cloud software company that helps businesses centralise and use contact, billing, contract and customer feedback data through services like its Sansan contact management platform, Bill One invoicing tool and Contract One solution, alongside its Eight business card app for individuals.

Operations: Sansan generates most of its revenue from its Sansan and Bill One segment at ¥46,847 million, with smaller contributions from the Eight Business at ¥6,720 million and Others at ¥415 million, almost entirely in Japan at ¥53,761 million.

Market Cap: ¥225.8b

Sansan catches the eye in this founder led screen because its contact and document cloud suite is backed by very strong earnings momentum, with profit margins moving into double digits and return on equity already above 30%. Management is pairing that profitability with shareholder returns through dividends and a ¥2,000 million buyback, while still targeting higher operating margins in the next financial year. At the same time, the stock carries a premium P/E and the share price has been volatile, and 100% of liabilities sit in higher risk funding sources.
